text: This is a video of a robot-assisted partial nephrectomy for right upper pole renal tumor.
The imaging reveals a 3 to 3.5 centimeter mass located at the right upper pole of the kidney, visibly abutting the liver.
So renal nephronometry score six K. So these are the four positions.
The surgical setup includes a camera port, two working ports, one port for retraction using the ProGrasp instrument, two assistant ports, and one port dedicated for liver retraction.
The surgeon begins mobilizing the ascending colon, using robotic instruments to carefully separate it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon performs caucarization of the duodenum, manipulating the tissue to separate it from surrounding structures.
You have to be very careful while using the diathermy in this step as it can cause duodenal injury.
The surgeon performs medialization of the gonadal vein, followed by lifting the ureter and the lower pole of the kidney, using surgical instruments to manipulate these structures.
The surgeon dissects parallel to the inferior vena cava while approaching the renal hilum, taking care to avoid injury to the surrounding structures.
The surgeon approaches the hilar structures, focusing on the careful dissection of the visible renal artery.
The surgeon dissects the renal artery and loops it with a vessel loop, followed by the dissection and looping of the renal vein.
The surgeon incises the peritoneum over the upper pole of the kidney to access the tumor. The kidney is mobilized, and the serotus fascia is opened to prepare for tumor resection.
The surgeon carefully resects the sticky fat surrounding the tumor, ensuring not to breach the tumor itself during the procedure.
The surgeon uses diathermy to score the renal capsule, marking the resection plane for the upcoming surgical steps.
At the renal hilum, the surgeon identifies another renal artery supplying the upper pole and loops it separately, ensuring clear visualization and control of the vessel.
Bulldog clamps are applied to the renal arteries and vein, occluding blood flow. Tumor resection begins using diathermy, with the surgeon gently pushing the tumor away from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon carefully enters the collecting system of the kidney, ensuring not to breach the tumor, while the bedside assistant provides suction, irrigation, and retraction support.

The surgeon develops a clear margin around the tumor, ensuring a safe distance from the tumor tissue. The lower margin is excised and sent for frozen section analysis to confirm the adequacy of the resection.

The surgeon begins the first layer of renal roughy using 3-0 B-lock sutures in a continuous fashion, securing each end with hemo locks. The sutures are brought through the renal capsule and the V log is secured with the hemo log.
The surgeon performs early unclamping of the renal vessels, indicating the end of the warm ischemia period, which lasted twenty minutes.

After early unclamping of the renal vessels, the surgeon proceeds with the second layer of suturing using V-lock 2-0 sutures and the sliding clip method. Vessel loops are repositioned, and flow sealant is applied to the area.

The resected tumor is placed into a specimen bag, and a drain is positioned in the surgical site. The final view shows the bivalved tumor specimen alongside multiple small incisions and a larger incision, approximately three centimeters, used for specimen retrieval.
